story:
I didn’t have a choice. Turning ten was happening with or without me. Everyone kept chirping “Double digits!” like I’d won a sparkly trophy, but I didn’t feel shiny. I felt nine. Nine-year-olds don’t have to do fractions. Nine-year-olds can eat dinosaur nuggets and not get blamed for cake disasters. Ten sounded suspiciously like trouble.

Mom had Big Plans: backyard blowout, streamers, balloons, a magician named Magic Marvin (balloon animals included), and a double-layer chocolate cake with blue frosting. “I just want it to be perfect!” she said, eyes twinkling with party-planning power. That’s when it all started.

I didn’t have a choice when I yanked down the “Party Stuff” box and it detonated like a birthday bomb. Streamers drifted like rainbow spaghetti. Glitter sneezed across the floor. I stepped on the “Happy Birthday” banner and ripped it in half. Mom ran in. “I was going to hang that in the tree!” “I didn’t have a choice,” I said, a streamer snake around my ankle.

Okay, the next part was maybe slightly my fault. Mom said, “Don’t touch the balloons.” So I only bounced one. But the others hovered there, tempting me like smiley bubble aliens. I grabbed last year’s pirate sword, and—POP. Pop-pop. POP-POP-POP. I stood holding a rubber sword in a blizzard of balloon guts. “I didn’t have a choice,” I told the dog. She looked unconvinced.

Magic Marvin was practicing a balloon poodle that looked like a snake with legs when he leaned toward me and said, “Those sticky notes—‘party canceled due to mysterious fog’, huh?” Mom whispered, “Go get the cake.” You know that feeling when you carry something too important and walk too slow and your arms start to quake? That. Halfway through the kitchen, I tripped over my own sock (80% the dog’s fault), and the cake flew like a blue-frosted UFO. It crash-landed. “NOOOOO!” screamed my cousin, who’d come early to help. “CAKE DOWN!” yelled Uncle Dan. I stared at my frosting-covered hands. “I didn’t have a choice.”

Also, there were too many people. Mom invited my whole class, the neighbors, and Mrs. Turnbull, who smells like cheese and doesn’t like kids. Panic made my brain fizz. I found Mom’s guest list and—okay—wrote “party canceled due to mysterious fog” on sticky notes and stealth-delivered them at school the day before. I slipped a few into the neighbors’ mailboxes, too. By 2 p.m., it was me, my dog, Magic Marvin, my cousin and Uncle Dan, and a hopeless balloon poodle. Mom sat on the steps. “Why would you do that?” “I didn’t have a choice,” I said, and even I knew that one was wobbly. Truth: I wanted to burrito myself in a blanket and read comics.

Upstairs, with blue frosting on my pants and a sad party hat, I thought about nine. Last year we had pizza, pin-the-tail-on-the-dinosaur, lopsided eyeball cupcakes, and Grandpa’s banjo song about socks turning into spaghetti. No explosions. No crying. The dog only ate one napkin. Nine was perfect. I wanted to go back. But I didn’t have a choice.

Maybe I did have a choice in what came next.

I marched outside. “Can we have a do-over? No magician, no balloons. Just us. Maybe… waffles?” Mom blinked. “Waffles?” “With sprinkles. And whipped cream. And chocolate chips.” “And guests?” “Just the ones who didn’t mind the fog.”

We had a Waffle Party. Magic Marvin stayed and made a balloon fork that deflated mid-bite. The dog wore a party hat like a crown and got syrup in her ear. Grandma’s card said, “Welcome to double digits!” with a confused donut. Uncle Dan yelled, “To the waffle warriors!” My cousin built a gummy-worm swamp stack. Mom made a smiley face. Mine had chocolate chips, rainbow sprinkles, and enough syrup to glue a shoe to the table. The dog sneezed whipped cream across the patio and we laughed so hard even Magic Marvin cracked a smile.

I still didn’t want to be ten. I still liked nine. But somewhere between the whipped cream and the syrup, I laughed for real. I didn’t have a choice about the mess or the year. I did have a choice to love the party—even the one I ruined. And honestly? It was better that way.
